questions.question: Explain the three timers used in RIP with their default values.
keyPoints: Update Timer (30 seconds): Periodic interval for sending routing updates
Invalid Timer (180 seconds): If no update received for 180s, route is marked invalid
Flush Timer (240 seconds): After invalid, route is removed from table after 240s

questions.question: What is Route Poisoning and how does it help in RIP?
keyPoints: Route poisoning is a technique where unreachable routes are advertised with metric 16
When a network becomes unreachable, router sets metric to 16 and advertises to all neighbors
This quickly propagates the 'bad news' about failed routes
Prevents routing loops by making infinity explicit

questions.question: Differentiate between RIPv1 and RIPv2.
keyPoints: RIPv1: Classful, no VLSM support, uses broadcast (255.255.255.255)
RIPv2: Classless, supports VLSM and CIDR, uses multicast (224.0.0.9)
RIPv2 includes subnet mask in updates, RIPv1 does not
RIPv2 has authentication feature, RIPv1 does not

questions.question: What is Split Horizon in RIP? Explain its purpose.
keyPoints: Split horizon is a rule that prevents a router from advertising routes back out the interface they were learned from
Purpose: Prevents routing loops in distance-vector protocols
If a route is advertised back to the source, it creates a loop
Also reduces unnecessary routing updates and bandwidth usage

questions.question: Explain the Count-to-Infinity problem in RIP.
keyPoints: Problem that occurs when a route becomes unreachable and routers have inconsistent information
Routes keep circulating between two routers, incrementing hop counts each time
Example: A→B→A→B... with hop counts increasing until they reach 16
Caused by slow convergence of distance-vector protocols
Solution: Define 16 as infinity (unreachable) and use split horizon, route poisoning

questions.question: What is Triggered Update in RIP? When does it occur?
keyPoints: Triggered updates are immediate routing updates sent when a route changes state
It occurs when: route becomes unreachable, route metric changes, interface state changes
Does not wait for the next 30-second periodic update
Helps in faster convergence compared to waiting for periodic updates

questions.question: List the advantages and limitations of RIP.
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to configure, widely supported, low overhead for small networks, uses UDP (lightweight)
Limitations: Slow convergence, maximum 15 hops restricts network size, uses only hop count (doesn't consider bandwidth), susceptible to routing loops, periodic updates waste bandwidth

questions.question: Describe the working of RIP in detail. Explain how routing updates are exchanged between routers.
keyPoints: RIP is a distance-vector protocol using hop count as metric
Routers maintain Routing Information Database (RIB)
Initial: Router learns directly connected networks (metric 0)
Update process: Every 30 seconds, router sends complete routing table to all neighbors
Receiving: Router adds 1 to received metrics, compares with existing routes
If new route better: Update routing table
If same destination via different next-hop: Keep existing (first come first serve)
If route via same next-hop: Update with new metric
Loop prevention: Split horizon, route poisoning, holddown timers
Convergence: When all routers have consistent routing information

questions.question: Explain the Bellman-Ford algorithm used in RIP. How does RIP implement this algorithm for path determination?
keyPoints: Bellman-Ford is a distance-vector algorithm that computes shortest paths
Basic principle: If distance to X is d, and neighbor Y is n hops away from X, then distance via Y is d+n
RIP implementation: Each router maintains distance vector (routing table)
Initialization: Direct connections = 0 hops, all other networks = ∞
Update iteration: Router exchanges distance vectors with neighbors
Bellman equation: D(x,y) = min { c(x,v) + D(v,y) } for all neighbors v
RIP simplifies: Each link has cost 1 (hop count), so D(x,y) = min { 1 + D(v,y) }
Process repeats until convergence (no changes in routing table)
RIP limit of 15 hops ensures algorithm terminates quickly

questions.question: Compare RIP with OSPF. Why is OSPF preferred over RIP in large networks?
keyPoints: RIP: Distance-vector, 15 hops max, 30s updates, simple, classful (v1)/classless (v2)
OSPF: Link-state, unlimited hops, 30min updates (or triggered), complex, classless
OSPF uses bandwidth as metric, RIP uses hop count
OSPF has faster convergence than RIP
OSPF uses Dijkstra's algorithm, RIP uses Bellman-Ford
OSPF supports load balancing across equal-cost paths
RIP periodic updates waste bandwidth; OSPF uses efficient flooding
OSPF supports area segmentation (hierarchical routing), RIP is flat
RIP susceptible to routing loops; OSPF uses SPF algorithm preventing loops
For large networks: OSPF scales better, converges faster, uses bandwidth efficiently

questions.question: A network has 4 routers A, B, C, and D connected in linear topology: A---B---C---D. If network 192.168.1.0/24 is connected to router A, explain how router D learns about this network through RIP. Include hop counts at each stage.
keyPoints: Initial state: Router A has network 192.168.1.0 with metric 0 (directly connected)
First update cycle: A advertises to B with metric 1 for network 192.168.1.0
Router B: Adds 1, updates table with metric 1, advertises to C with metric 2
Router C: Receives from B with metric 2, updates table, advertises to D with metric 3
Router D: Receives from C with metric 3, updates table with 192.168.1.0 via C, metric 3
This is optimal path: D→C→B→A = 3 hops
If any link fails, triggered updates propagate changes
All routers converge with consistent routing information after 30-240 seconds
Maximum is 15 hops; any path exceeding 15 is unreachable
